question: run_stage4.py wraps Stage 4 (fetch fix branch, show diff/artifact summary, merge to main, drop _harness/) and must NOT auto-merge on its own — Morley reviews every merge. What's the right mechanism to enforce that: (a) an interactive terminal confirmation prompt the skill blocks on, (b) a required --confirm flag with no interactive fallback, or (c) a two-step dry-run/apply split, where the first call only shows the diff and writes a pending-merge marker file, and a second call with --apply performs the actual merge? Weigh how this skill will actually be invoked (from an agent's own tool-call loop, not a human typing at a terminal) against the risk of an agent talking itself into supplying whatever confirmation is needed.
synthesis: Implement option (c): split run_stage4.py into two distinct invocations. The first (default) call fetches the fix branch, computes the diff/artifact summary, and writes a pending-merge marker file (keyed to the run/branch) — it never merges. The second call, invoked only with an explicit --apply flag, reads that marker, re-validates it still matches the current branch state, performs the merge to main, and drops _harness/. No interactive prompt and no bare --confirm flag should be used: prompts don't work in a non-TTY agent tool-call loop (and can be trivially satisfied via piped stdin), and a same-call --confirm flag is just as easy for an agent to self-supply as the action it's meant to gate. The two-step split is the right mechanism because it forces a hard boundary between 'summarize' and 'mutate' as two separate tool invocations, produces a concrete artifact (the marker) that documents what was reviewed and when, and gives Morley (or any wrapping orchestration/policy layer) a real point to inspect before the second call happens. This is the minimal in-script mechanism that matches how the skill is actually invoked (agent tool-call loop, not a terminal) while still creating an auditable review boundary.
contradictions: Whether the --apply capability should be physically excluded from the agent's own toolset (only Morley can invoke it) versus the agent being allowed to call both dry-run and --apply itself, relying on the split merely to force a pause/audit trail rather than a hard access boundary — the advisors disagree on this without it being resolved in the question's scope.
blindspots: No mechanism inside run_stage4.py itself can prove Morley actually reviewed the diff — an agent with general shell access can call the dry-run step and then immediately call --apply in the same loop.

A stale or mismatched pending-merge marker file (e.g., from an aborted run, or if the branch changes between the two calls) could be consumed by a later --apply, merging the wrong state; the apply step must re-validate the marker against current branch/diff state before merging.

If the agent's toolset is meant to exclude --apply entirely (per Advisor 1's stricter reading), that is an access-control/deployment decision outside run_stage4.py's own logic and must be enforced by whoever wires up the agent's available tools — the script cannot enforce this on its own.

True enforcement of human review ultimately depends on controls outside this script (e.g., restricting which invocations are exposed to the agent, or platform-level review gates); the marker-file split reduces accidental/same-turn auto-merge risk but is not a complete authority boundary.

question: How thoroughly should this build verify itself before emitting BUILD_COMPLETE? Options range from cheap (python -m py_compile on everything, confirm the plugin manifests are valid JSON) to moderate (start `hermes dashboard` on loopback, confirm it boots and the three tabs are discoverable via its plugin-list API) to thorough (also drive each harness-control skill through a harmless dry-run path, e.g. run_stage1 --help or an equivalent no-op check). Given this build has no automated test suite of its own and no swarm/critic pass will run against it, pick the verification depth that actually catches real breakage without inventing work with no signal.
synthesis: Verify at the moderate depth, treated as a floor rather than a ceiling: run python -m py_compile across all source, validate every plugin manifest as JSON, then start `hermes dashboard` on loopback, confirm it boots cleanly, and confirm all three tabs are discoverable via the plugin-list API. Do not add per-skill dry-run/--help invocations as a required gate for BUILD_COMPLETE. Rationale: the cheap checks catch nothing beyond syntax/format and would let real integration breakage (import failures, registration bugs, dead entrypoints) slip through, so they're necessary but not sufficient. The dashboard-boot + plugin-list check exercises the actual integration surface that matters here — the runtime loading and registration path that both the build and its plugins depend on — and is the highest-signal check available given there's no test suite and no critic pass to backstop deeper claims. Extending to thorough (dry-running each harness-control skill) adds execution time and environment-coupling (ports, CLI paths, permissions) for marginal signal: a `--help`/no-op path mostly proves argument parsing works, not that the skill's real logic is sound, so it risks manufacturing false confidence dressed up as rigor. If skill dry-runs are truly free, already exist as documented no-op paths, and cost negligible time, running them is not harmful — but they should not be treated as a required signal for BUILD_COMPLETE, since their absence doesn't materially change what's actually verified about correctness.
contradictions: Advisors 1, 2, and 3 hold that dry-running harness-control skills is low-signal busywork that should be excluded; Advisor 4 holds that omitting skill dry-runs leaves CLI entrypoint breakage completely unchecked and that the extra step is cheap, harmless, and worth doing given the total absence of a test suite.

Disagreement on how much weight to give 'no test suite exists' as justification for extra verification breadth versus using it as a reason to avoid inventing checks that don't map to a real test contract.
blindspots: Even moderate (or thorough) verification cannot detect that a skill's real invocation logic works — a plugin can register cleanly and appear in the plugin-list API yet crash the moment it's actually run with real arguments, hardware, or state.
The plugin-list API only confirms backend registration; it says nothing about whether the frontend actually renders the three tabs or whether their UI is functional.

Booting a loopback dashboard and executing CLI commands during the build introduces environment sensitivity (port conflicts, path/permission issues) that can produce flaky, non-deterministic BUILD_COMPLETE failures unrelated to real code breakage.

No mechanism in this build re-validates any of this over time — moderate verification confirms health at build time only, with no regression protection afterward since there is no automated test suite or critic pass.
